key_insight: A unified microscopic constitutive model links nonaffine elasticity and structural relaxation to explain stress overshoot, yielding, steady plastic flow, and polymer strain hardening in amorphous solids.
engineering_relevance: The theory offers a physically grounded way to predict full stress--strain curves for metallic and polymer glasses across strain rates using a small set of measurable parameters.
method: Combines nonaffine elastic response with irreversible many-body relaxation.
Uses stretched-exponential relaxation as the main dynamical input from modulus or stress relaxation data.
Includes finite chain extensibility to capture strain hardening in polymer glasses.
evidence_signal: The model quantitatively reproduces experimental stress--strain curves for metallic glasses, polycarbonate, PMMA, and epoxy resins over a broad range of strain rates.
watch_item: Applicability may depend on having reliable relaxation measurements and may be less direct for materials outside the tested amorphous glass classes.
paper_title: Microscopic constitutive theory of stress overshoot, yielding, and strain hardening in amorphous materials
